segments.title: Global infrastructure as naval strength
segments.summary: The Royal Navy's worldwide bases are both the foundation of global warfighting and a peacetime financial burden.
segments.body: Clarke frames overseas infrastructure as an asset that must be maintained before a crisis, not improvised during one. A navy can only exploit global reach if its bases, repair facilities, and support capacity survive the austerity of peacetime budgets.

evidence.note: The lecture contrasts the advantages and maintenance costs of worldwide Royal Navy infrastructure.

segments.title: Dock capacity shapes practical ship size
segments.summary: Repair spaces sized for 15,000- to 20,000-ton ships made that range especially efficient for Royal Navy force design.
segments.body: The discussion connects infrastructure to warship design rather than treating docks as an afterthought. Treaty limits left a gap between cruisers and battleships, but existing repair capacity made ships in the middle range easier to sustain efficiently across the network.

evidence.note: Clarke links available Royal Navy maintenance space with the useful size of heavy cruisers.

segments.title: Commercial docks do not guarantee strategic coverage
segments.summary: Civilian dry docks followed trade routes, while a global navy needed repair capacity where strategy demanded it.
segments.body: Commercial facilities could be plentiful on well-travelled routes yet unavailable, distant, or politically uncertain for naval use. Clarke uses the Atlantic and routes beyond India to show why a Royal Navy plan could not assume friendly commercial infrastructure would always solve its support problem.

evidence.note: The lecture contrasts civilian dock locations with naval strategic requirements and contingency planning.

segments.title: Why Bermuda received a floating dock
segments.summary: A floating dock at Bermuda could support a significant squadron while remaining an asset Britain could relocate.
segments.body: The Bermuda dock combined repair capacity with political and strategic flexibility: unlike a graving dock, it could be moved if circumstances changed. Its central and defensible position let ships avoid a return to Britain or dependence on more distant North American and Caribbean facilities for routine maintenance.

evidence.note: Clarke explains floating-dock capacity, mobility, and Bermuda's location on the North America station.

segments.title: Towing a dock across the Atlantic
segments.summary: The first Bermuda dock was built to be towed to station, turning the transfer itself into a major naval operation.
segments.body: Clarke describes a dock designed to handle ironclads of up to 10,000 tons and to support vessels longer than the dock itself. Its Atlantic tow at roughly 4.8 knots required multiple large warships and illustrates that mobile infrastructure still demands careful protection, planning, and seamanship to deploy.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses the dock's towing force, dimensions, capacity, and Atlantic passage.

segments.title: Preventive maintenance makes a fleet real
segments.summary: Floating docks made hull inspection and preventive work possible away from home, turning nominal fleet numbers into usable readiness.
segments.body: A floating dock could lift a ship clear of the water for hull work and could also be careened for its own maintenance. Clarke argues that fleet strength is not the number of vessels on a list but the number with current capability that are actually maintained and ready for operations.

evidence.note: The lecture explains docking, hull maintenance, preventive replacement, and the distinction between inventory and readiness.

segments.title: The cost of keeping a floating dock afloat
segments.summary: Floating docks reduce ships' long-term repair costs, but they demand their own intensive maintenance in salt water.
segments.body: Capacity was deliberately matched to the ships a dock was expected to handle, rather than simply maximized. Unlike a fixed graving dock, a floating structure must be inspected and repaired like a ship while repeatedly flooding and drying, making corrosion and heavy-use damage central operating concerns.

evidence.note: Clarke compares docking costs and explains why a floating dock itself needs continual upkeep.

segments.title: Upgrading dock capacity by adding sections
segments.summary: Bermuda's later floating dock shows how a structure could be enlarged and upgraded more readily than a fixed graving dock.
segments.body: The 1902 dock gained capacity through extensions and later work, eventually handling far more than its original design lift. Clarke contrasts adding a fabricated floating section with the major excavation, land acquisition, and engineering needed to enlarge a fixed dock, while stressing that the apparently simple work remained complex naval engineering.

evidence.note: The transcript traces the Bermuda dock's growth, extensions, testing, and service before deployment.

segments.title: A worldwide ladder of dock capacity
segments.summary: The Admiralty used floating docks of many sizes, from destroyer lifts to facilities capable of handling HMS Hood.
segments.body: Clarke maps a distributed fleet-support system in which small docks prevented destroyers from competing with capital ships for scarce large facilities. Large docks at Malta and Singapore supported the biggest units, while mobile capacity could be built in Britain and shifted to places such as Reykjavik or Sydney as requirements changed.

evidence.note: The lecture compares Admiralty floating docks by capacity, location, and the ship types they sustained.

segments.title: Repair resilience matters as much as carrier numbers
segments.summary: A force's apparent size can conceal a single-point repair failure, especially when a major warship has only one realistic dock option.
segments.body: Clarke applies the historical lesson to the modern Royal Navy: carrier availability depends on the resilience of maintenance capacity, not merely on the number of hulls. A dock accident or an occupied facility can reduce nominal capability sharply during a period of tension short of war, when emergency access to commercial space may not be available.

evidence.note: Clarke uses current carrier repair capacity to explain the difference between paper and resilient availability.

segments.title: Infrastructure constrains capital-ship design
segments.summary: The availability of docks shaped the designs the Royal Navy could realistically build, maintain, and deploy.
segments.body: HMS Hood demonstrates how a large ship's usefulness depended on access to suitable floating docks around the world. Clarke connects delayed infrastructure investment after the First World War to the design pressure on later capital ships: naval architects had to obtain as much fighting value as possible within the limits of existing and plausibly funded dock capacity.

evidence.note: The transcript links Hood, delayed dock investment, and capital-ship design constraints.

segments.title: Mobile infrastructure sustains maritime power
segments.summary: Floating docks made repair capacity deployable, giving the Admiralty unusual control over where fleet maintenance could occur.
segments.body: The concluding argument treats a floating dock as infrastructure that can be built in one place and moved to another rather than permanently tied to a single harbour. That mobility gave the Royal Navy a way to sustain ships near an operational theatre and offers a broader lesson: maintenance capacity and time for inspection are indispensable parts of any complex fleet system.

evidence.note: Clarke summarizes mobile dock infrastructure and relates maintenance discipline to future complex transport systems.
